entries.name: thm:baseline-lift
entries.statement:
[Baseline lift for $\Psi_i$, $\Theta$, and $V_i$]\label{thm:baseline-lift}
Assume $\alpha>3/4$. Let
\begin{equation*}
\Psi_i=I_i(\xi_i),
\qquad
\Theta=\Psi_1\Psi_2,
\qquad
V_i=I_i(\Theta).
\end{equation*}
Then the cutoff approximations converge almost surely in the path spaces
\begin{equation}\label{eq:baseline-paths}
\Psi_i\in C_T\mathcal{C}^{\alpha-3/2-},
\qquad
\Theta\in C_T\mathcal{C}^{2\alpha-3-},
\qquad
V_i\in C_T\mathcal{C}^{3\alpha-3-},
\qquad
\partial_tV_i\in C_T\mathcal{C}^{2\alpha-3-}.
\end{equation}
In particular, $\Theta$ is a cross-Wick product without subtracting a scalar constant.

entries.name: prop:cubic-quartic-baseline
thm:operator-main
prop:mid-completion
thm:conditional-closure-intro
cor:conditional-cutoff
entries.statement:
[Conditional convergence of the cutoff equations]\label{cor:conditional-cutoff}
If the cutoff enhanced data converge in the norms required by Theorem \ref{thm:conditional-closure-intro} and are uniformly bounded on a common time interval, then the cutoff solutions of \eqref{eq:cutoff-system} converge to the paracontrolled solution. The limiting formulation contains no singular random-operator centering term.

entries.name: rem:status-emphasis
entries.statement:
[Status and emphasis]\label{rem:status-emphasis}
The unconditional core of the paper is Theorem \ref{thm:baseline-lift}, Proposition \ref{prop:cubic-quartic-baseline}, Theorem \ref{thm:operator-main}, and Proposition \ref{prop:mid-completion}. The full stochastic Cauchy problem is conditional only because one still has to choose a closure route for the cubic forcing $F^V$ and insert an appropriate fractional periodic Klein--Gordon Strichartz theorem. In particular, the random-operator part is already complete at the level relevant to the no singular centering mechanism.

entries.name: prop:sharpness
remark_9
entries.statement:
[Why $V_i$ is not inserted into the $L^2$ input slot]
A basic consistency check is that the random operators act on $U_1$ and $U_2$, not on $V_1$ and $V_2$. In the fractional range $\alpha<1$, the first Picard objects $V_i$ have negative baseline regularity $3\alpha-3-$ and should be treated as part of the enhanced stochastic data, not as $L^2$ inputs of the random-operator theorem.

entries.name: remark_10
entries.statement:
[What is already complete here]
At the level of canonical stochastic distributions, the cubic and quartic forcing terms are not the main difficulty. The already-integrated pieces enjoy additional phase geometry, and the lower-chaos contraction channels are finite. The real difficulty appears only if one insists on inserting the whole forcing into the conservative source norm $C_TH^{s_2-\alpha}$.

entries.name: lem:centered-cov
entries.proof:
[Proof sketch]
If $j\neq k$, independence gives zero mean and Wick's theorem leaves one free high-frequency variable, hence $O(N^3)$ possibilities. The squared Duhamel kernel contributes $N^{-2\alpha}$ and the two covariances contribute $N^{-4\alpha}$, yielding \eqref{eq:centered-cov}. If $j=k$, the only genuine diagonal contraction is exactly the term removed in \eqref{eq:B-kernel}; the remaining centered second chaos has the same shell count. Time increments are obtained by placing the increment on the Duhamel kernel, the terminal stochastic convolution, or the same-color subtraction.

entries.name: thm:pathwise-fluct
lem:speed-gap
lem:volterra
prop:det-contraction
entries.proof:
[Proof sketch]
The lower-order terms in \eqref{eq:sigma-explicit} are absolutely summable and contribute $N^{3-4\alpha}$. For the leading covariance term, the shell count is $N^3$, the Duhamel kernel contributes $N^{-\alpha}$, and the covariance amplitude contributes $N^{-2\alpha}$. Lemma \ref{lem:speed-gap} shows that both phases in \eqref{eq:phases-pm} are bounded below by $N^\alpha$, so Lemma \ref{lem:volterra} yields one more denominator $N^{-\alpha}$. This gives the main shell $N^{3-4\alpha}$. If the derivative falls on the input $w$, the low output frequency satisfies $|q|\le N^\theta$ and the conversion from $H^{-\alpha-\varepsilon}$ to the output weight $H^{s_2-\alpha}$ costs exactly $N^{\theta(s_2+\varepsilon)}$.

entries.name: thm:no-singular-centering
ass:strichartz
remark_18
entries.statement: For $\alpha=1$ this is the usual local wave/Klein--Gordon package. For fractional flows on compact manifolds one may need derivative losses; if so, the closure inequalities below must be shifted accordingly. The present paper keeps this input explicit.

entries.name: def:admissible
entries.statement:
[Admissible deterministic parameters]\label{def:admissible}
We call $(\alpha,s_1,s_2,\theta)$ admissible for the conservative source formulation if, for sufficiently small $\varepsilon>0$,
\begin{align}
&0<s_1<s_2,
\qquad 0<\theta<1, \label{eq:adm-1}\\
&s_2<4\alpha-3-\frac32\theta-\varepsilon,
\qquad 3-4\alpha+\theta(s_2+\varepsilon)<0, \label{eq:adm-2}\\
&s_1<2\alpha-\frac32-\varepsilon, \label{eq:adm-3}\\
&s_2>\frac32-\alpha+\varepsilon,
\qquad s_2<s_1+2\alpha-\frac32-\varepsilon, \label{eq:adm-4}\\
&s_2\le s_1+\frac\alpha4, \label{eq:adm-5}\\
&\rho_V+s_1>0,
\qquad s_2-\alpha<\rho_V. \label{eq:adm-6}
\end{align}

entries.name: remark_20
entries.statement: The first line in \eqref{eq:adm-2} is the centered fluctuation condition, the second line in \eqref{eq:adm-2} is the same-color deterministic contraction condition, \eqref{eq:adm-3} is the naive low--high mapping for the $X$-equation, \eqref{eq:adm-4} comes from the basic product theory for $U>\Psi$ and $Y\circ\Psi$, \eqref{eq:adm-5} is the quadratic Strichartz closure, and \eqref{eq:adm-6} treats the products $V_iU_j$.

entries.name: rem:primitive-route
entries.statement:
[Primitive route]\label{rem:primitive-route}
The deterministic theorem above is stated in the conservative source formulation because it is the cleanest closed statement. For lowering the full SPDE threshold, however, the more natural route is to construct the primitives $G_i^V=I_i(F^V)$ directly in the solution space. This should exploit oscillatory geometry in the already-integrated cubic forcing that is invisible in the static source norm. The resulting enhanced formulation is deeper, because one must then control additional products involving $G_i^V$.

entries.name: cor:safe-range
entries.proof: Choose $s_2=\frac32-\alpha+\eta$ with $\eta>0$ small. From \eqref{eq:adm-5} one needs $s_1>s_2-\alpha/4=\frac32-\frac54\alpha+\eta$, while \eqref{eq:adm-3} requires $s_1<2\alpha-\frac32-\varepsilon$. These inequalities are compatible precisely when $\alpha>12/13$ up to losses. Since $12/13>9/10$, the operator-level upper bound \eqref{eq:adm-2} can then be met for small $\theta$.
